27: A direct revelation: 'And I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes and be careful to obey my rules.' Pentecost is Ezekiel 36:27 enacted. The Spirit's indwelling is not merely motivational but causally efficacious: 'I will cause you to walk' — the Hebrew Hiphil form makes YHWH the enabling cause of the obedience that follows. This is the new covenant's answer to the old covenant's demand without the enabling Spirit: the same Torah-standard now fulfilled because the Spirit from within enables what the law from without could only command. Paul's 'the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit' (Romans 8:4) is the Christological-pneumatological fulfillment of Ezekiel 36:27.
1: Chapter 36 is one of the most Christologically rich chapters in Ezekiel. The chapter begins with restoration oracles to the mountains of Israel — the land itself addressed as a covenantal partner. Christologically, the restoration of the land is the material dimension of the new creation: "the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption" (Romans 8:21). Christ's redemptive work is not merely spiritual but encompasses the physical creation (Romans 8:19-23; Revelation 21:1-5).
2: The enemies who said the ancient heights have become their possession. The nations' claim over the covenant land represents the anti-Kingdom forces. Christologically, the reversal of enemy possession of the covenant promises is accomplished in Christ: "the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet" (Romans 16:20). What the enemy claimed is reclaimed through the one who disarmed the powers (Colossians 2:15).
3: Made desolate and swallowed up by the nations — the covenant land despised. The cosmic scandal of the covenant promise appearing to fail. Christologically, the apparent failure of the covenant at the cross (the Messiah crucified, the promises appearing void) is the moment when the deepest purpose of the covenant is accomplished. "He was despised and rejected by men" (Isaiah 53:3) — and through that despising came the restoration of all things.
4: YHWH speaks to the mountains, hills, ravines, and valleys — the comprehensive geographical address. The cosmic scope of the restoration: every feature of the land is included. Christologically, the new creation is equally comprehensive: "all things were created through him and for him... and in him all things hold together" (Colossians 1:16-17). The one who holds the physical creation is the one through whom its restoration comes.
5: YHWH speaks in fiery jealousy against the rest of the nations, especially Edom. Divine jealousy as the protective love that refuses to let the covenant promises be permanently negated. Christologically, this protective love is expressed in Jesus's temple cleansing: "zeal for your house will consume me" (John 2:17 citing Psalms 69:9) — the jealous protection of the Father's covenantal house.
6: The fire of YHWH's jealousy spoken to the land of Israel. The prophetic passion for the covenant's fulfillment drives the restoration oracle. Christologically, the divine jealousy for the covenant people is the background for Paul's statement: "I feel a divine jealousy for you, since I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ" (2 Corinthians 11:2). The apostolic mission shares the divine jealousy for the covenant community.
7: YHWH's oath to make the nations bear their shame. The divine oath as the guarantee of covenant fulfillment. Christologically, God's oath is the foundation of sure hope: "when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose, he guaranteed it with an oath" (Hebrews 6:17). The oath to restore Israel is as secure as the oath of redemption through Christ.
8: The mountains of Israel shall shoot forth branches and yield fruit for the people Israel — because they are soon to come home. The fruitfulness of the restored land as the sign of homecoming. Christologically, the branches bearing fruit on the mountains of Israel echo Jesus's vine-and-branches teaching (John 15:1-8): the eschatological fruitfulness of the land is realized spiritually in the union of believers with Christ, and will be realized physically in the new creation.
9: YHWH is for the mountains — turning toward them to till and sow. The divine commitment to the physical restoration of the land. Christologically, the God who turns toward the barren mountains is the Father who runs toward the returning prodigal son (Luke 15:20). The divine turning-toward is the initiating act of all restoration.
10: Multiplying people on the mountains — the cities inhabited, the waste places rebuilt. The demographic restoration: the people returning and filling the land. Christologically, the great commission ("make disciples of all nations") is the eschatological people-multiplication: the church as the new Israel whose numbers fill the earth with the knowledge of God (Habakkuk 2:14; Matthew 28:19).
11: More people and animals than before, prosperous and more numerous than formerly. The restoration exceeds the original — the new creation is greater than the first creation. Christologically, the NT pattern is consistent: Christ gives more than what was lost. "Last Adam" gives more than "first Adam" lost (Romans 5:15: "the free gift is not like the trespass... how much more have the grace of God and the free gift by the grace of that one man Jesus Christ abounded for many").
12: People walking on the mountains, the land no longer bereaving the nation. The land that "ate" its people (Ezekiel 36:13-14) is now a nurturing land. Christologically, the reversal from devouring land to life-giving land mirrors the resurrection: death that devoured life (1 Corinthians 15:26: "the last enemy to be destroyed is death") is swallowed up by resurrection victory (1 Corinthians 15:54-55: "death is swallowed up in victory").
13: The accusation: "You devour men and bereave your nation of children." The land itself accused of consuming its people — the covenantal reversal where the promised land became the devouring land. Christologically, the curse of the devouring land (Leviticus 26:38: "the land shall devour you") is taken by Christ: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13). The devouring curse is absorbed by the one who was devoured by death and rose again.
14: No more devouring men, no more bereaving the nation — the taunt of the nations ended. The restorative promise for the land. Christologically, the ending of the taunt corresponds to the silencing of the accuser: "the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down" (Revelation 12:10). Christ's victory ends the enemy's claim to use the covenant curses against the covenant people.
15: No more hearing the reproach of the nations, no longer causing stumbling to the nations. The restoration of covenant honor before the watching world. Christologically, the church's calling is to be a community that displays the glory of Christ rather than causing the nations to reproach: "live honorably among the Gentiles, so that... they may see your good deeds and glorify God on the day of visitation" (1 Peter 2:12).
16: The word of YHWH comes with the explanation of why the exile happened. The oracle pivots from the land's restoration to the theological rationale. Christologically, the reason for the exile (covenant unfaithfulness producing judgment) is the exact situation that the cross addresses: "God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them" (2 Corinthians 5:19).
17: The house of Israel defiled the land by their conduct — like the defilement of a woman's menstrual impurity. The strongest language of ritual and moral defilement. Christologically, the comprehensive defilement of the covenant community is precisely the condition that requires the all-sufficient cleansing of Christ's blood: "the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin" (1 John 1:7) — not partial cleansing but comprehensive purification of all defilement.
18: Pouring out wrath on them because of blood, idols, and defilement. The multiple-category covenant violation. Christologically, the multi-category transgression requires a multi-dimensional redemption: "in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses" (Ephesians 1:7), covering all categories of offense against the covenant.
19: Scattered among the nations — judged according to their conduct and deeds. The exile as covenant judgment. Christologically, the scattering of the covenant people creates the precondition for the gathering: "Jesus would die for the nation, and not for the nation only, but also to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad" (John 11:51-52). The scattering is not the end but the preparation for the universal ingathering.
20: The profanation of YHWH's name among the nations — "these are YHWH's people, yet they have gone out of his land." The theological scandal: the people of YHWH experiencing covenant judgment appeared to prove YHWH's powerlessness or faithlessness to the nations. Christologically, the scandal of the cross is the same: "Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles" (1 Corinthians 1:23). Yet this apparent scandal is "the power of God and the wisdom of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24).
21: YHWH has concern for his holy name. The divine motivation for restoration: not primarily the people's merit but YHWH's own reputation and character. Christologically, this is the basis of salvation by grace alone: "to the praise of his glorious grace" (Ephesians 1:6) — the ultimate purpose of redemption is the display of YHWH's own glory and character, not the reward of human merit.
22: Not for the sake of the house of Israel but for the sake of the holy name. The explicitly grace-basis of the restoration — merit excluded entirely. Christologically, this is the justification-by-faith principle: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:23-24). Not for our sake but for the sake of the one who redeems.
23: Sanctifying the great name profaned among the nations — the nations will know YHWH. The universal scope of the name-vindication: all nations will see the covenant kept. Christologically, the sanctification of YHWH's name is what Christ accomplishes on the cross. The Lord's Prayer opens with "hallowed be your name" — the petition that the cross answers definitively, as God shows himself both just and the justifier (Romans 3:26).
24: The new Exodus gathering: "I will take you from the nations and gather you from all the countries and bring you into your own land." The eschatological new Exodus — the greatest ingathering. Christologically, this is fulfilled both in the return from exile and ultimately in Christ's universal gathering: "I will draw all people to myself" (John 12:32); "he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds" (Matthew 24:31). The gathering from all nations through Christ fulfills the scope of Ezekiel 36:24.
25: The cleansing waters: "I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean from all your uncleannesses, and from all your idols I will cleanse you." The sprinkling of clean water — Christologically the most direct OT anticipation of Christian baptism. The Num 19 purification-water ritual (sprinkled for corpse-impurity) is here elevated to a universal eschatological purification: all uncleannesses and all idols. Titus 3:5: "the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit"; 1 Corinthians 6:11: "washed... sanctified... justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ." The baptismal waters of the new covenant fulfill the sprinkling of Ezekiel 36:25.
26: The new heart and new spirit: "And I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh." This is the most direct OT statement of regeneration — the new birth Jesus describes in John 3:3-8. The heart of stone (impermeable to divine influence) replaced by the heart of flesh (responsive to YHWH) is the work of the Spirit through the gospel. Paul: "you are a letter from Christ... written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Corinthians 3:3). The stony law-tablets replaced by the fleshy-heart Spirit-tablets — Ezekiel 36:26 in Pauline register.
28: Covenant renewal: you shall be my people: "You shall dwell in the land that I gave to your fathers, and you shall be my people, and I will be your God." The covenant formula (ani YHWH eloheikhem) — "I will be your God and you shall be my people" — running through Leviticus 26:12, Jeremiah 31:33, Ezekiel 36:28, is the heartbeat of the covenant. Christologically, this covenant formula is fulfilled in the new covenant community: "I will be their God and they shall be my people" (Hebrews 8:10, quoting Jeremiah 31:33); "I will dwell in them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people" (2 Corinthians 6:16); ultimately in Revelation 21:3: "the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God."
29: Delivering from uncleanness, summoning grain and multiplying it. The complete restoration of agricultural abundance. Christologically, the abundance promised here points to the new creation's fruitfulness: the earth that cursed the first Adam (Genesis 3:17-19) is released from its curse in Christ (Revelation 22:3: "no longer will there be anything accursed"). The grain multiplication anticipates the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:1-14) — the messianic sign of covenant abundance.
30: Fruit of the tree and produce of the field multiplied — no more famine among the nations. The reversal of the covenant curse (Leviticus 26:20: "your land shall not yield its produce") through covenant restoration. Christologically, the bread-of-life teaching (John 6:35: "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger") is the spiritual fulfillment of the grain and fruit abundance that Ezekiel 36:30 promises for the new covenant era.
31: Remembering evil ways and deeds that were not good, loathing yourselves. The shame of restoration — not triumphalism but genuine self-knowledge in the light of grace. Christologically, the post-resurrection community of Peter and the disciples models this: Peter wept bitterly after the denial (Luke 22:62); the post-resurrection meeting includes restoration despite shame (John 21:15-19). Grace creates self-knowledge, not self-congratulation.
32: Not for your sake: "It is not for your sake that I will act, declares the Lord YHWH; let that be known to you. Be ashamed and confounded for your ways, O house of Israel." The double negation of human merit: not for your sake, and shame is the appropriate response to grace. Christologically, this is the heart of the theology of grace: salvation is entirely YHWH's initiative, for YHWH's glory, so that human shame before grace produces gratitude rather than pride. "By grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast" (Ephesians 2:8-9).
33: On the day of cleansing, cities inhabited and waste places rebuilt. The day of cleansing as the beginning of restoration. Christologically, "the day of cleansing" is the cross: "the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin" (1 John 1:7) — the day of ultimate cleansing inaugurates the building of the new temple community. "You are being built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit" (Ephesians 2:22).
34: The desolate land tilled instead of being a desolation in the sight of all who passed by. The restoration visible to observers. Christologically, the church as the renewed covenant community is visible evidence of divine restoration: "you are a letter from Christ... to be known and read by all" (2 Corinthians 3:2-3). The tilled-and-fruitful land = the Spirit-transformed community of believers whose lives are the visible sign of the new covenant's reality.
35: Like the Garden of Eden: "They will say: this land that was desolate has become like the garden of Eden, and the waste and desolate and ruined cities are now inhabited and fortified." The Eden comparison is the new-creation comparison: the restored land surpasses the original creation by being explicitly compared to Eden. Christologically, Christ as the last Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45) restores and surpasses what the first Adam lost. The new Jerusalem of Revelation 21-22 is explicitly Eden-imagery: the river of life, the tree of life, the removal of the curse — Ezekiel 36:35 is the transitional promise between Eden and the New Jerusalem.
36: The nations around knowing that YHWH rebuilt and replanted. The universal recognition of the divine work. Christologically, the church among the nations is the ongoing sign of YHWH's rebuilding: "by this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another" (John 13:35). The covenant community's visible transformation is the evidence that makes the nations say "YHWH has done this."
37: YHWH's declaration: "I have spoken and I will do it." The absolute divine commitment — word and action unified. Christologically, Christ is the Word become flesh (John 1:14) — the ultimate unity of divine word and divine action. The promise that YHWH speaks and will perform is fulfilled in the one who is himself both the promise and its fulfillment: "all the promises of God find their Yes in him" (2 Corinthians 1:20).
38: Israel praying for the rebuilding — "let the house of Israel pray for this to be done for them." The prayer of the covenant community as the mechanism of receiving the promised restoration. Christologically, this grounds the eschatological promises in prayer: "Ask, and it will be given to you" (Matthew 7:7); "the prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working" (James 5:16). The church prays "your kingdom come" (Matthew 6:10) as the corporate Ezekiel 36:37 prayer — requesting the new-creation fullness that YHWH has promised to give.
